content:
I've been thinking more about custom formatting, and I wonder if this approach could work:

use core::fmt;

/// Formatter that you can write invalid utf-8 to.
struct KernelFormatter<'a> {
    inner: fmt::Formatter<'a>,
}

impl<'a> KernelFormatter<'a> {
    unsafe fn new<'b>(f: &'b mut fmt::Formatter<'a>) -> &'b mut Self {
        unsafe { &mut *(f as *mut _ as *mut Self) }
    }

    fn write_bytes(&mut self, byt: &[u8]) -> fmt::Result {
        self.inner.write_str(unsafe { core::str::from_utf8_unchecked(byt) })
    }
}

/// Does not assume that `write_str` is passed values containing valid utf-8.
unsafe trait KernelWrite: core::fmt::Write {
    fn write_bytes(&mut self, byt: &[u8]) -> fmt::Result {
        self.write_str(unsafe { core::str::from_utf8_unchecked(byt) })
    }
}

trait KernelDisplay {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut KernelFormatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result;
}



struct FmtAdapter<T>(T);

impl<T: KernelDisplay> core::fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<T> {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        self.0.fmt(unsafe { KernelFormatter::new(f) })
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Our printing macros just wrap every argument in FmtAdapter
    // and otherwise pass through to built-in formatting logic.
    println!("{}", FmtAdapter(17));
}

Then, we implement our own KernelDisplay trait for the types we wish to be able to print. This lets us print core::str::CStr directly, while also letting us remove the ability to print raw pointers entirely. Thoughts?

cc @Tamir Duberstein
This could help with the CStr conundrum

Alice Ryhl said:

Then, we implement our own KernelDisplay trait for the types we wish to be able to print. This lets us print core::str::CStr directly, while also letting us remove the ability to print raw pointers entirely. Thoughts?

There are two separate proposals here, right? The first is FmtAdapter + KernelDisplay which achieve the goals you listed, and the second is KernelFormatter which is about not assuming UTF-8. Right?
Hmm, I guess you're right. I had not realized they could be split like that - though I believe that not assuming UTF-8 depends on the other stuff.
Cc @Gary Guo since IIRC he had some ideas early on (i.e. years ago) when we were discussing custom formatting/printing.

Well, a custom trait is the classic solution to the orphan problem, so this makes sense to me. I agree it digs us out of the CStr hole. One problem is that new Display impls in core could cause our blanket KernelDisplay impl to overlap with manual impls. I would imagine we're already exposed to breakage of this kind, though.
Oh, and the manual impls would have to be on FmtAdapter<&CStr> rather than CStr, right?
No the manual impl would be impl KernelDisplay for CStr.
There shouldn't be any orpahn issue here because the trait is declared in the kernel crate, which is also where the impl goes.
Core can't add new impls that would overlap with impl<T: KernelDisplay> core::fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<T> because core promises no breaking changes.

impl KernelDisplay for i32 {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut KernelFormatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        fmt::Display::fmt(self, &mut f.inner)
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Our printing macros just wrap every argument in FmtAdapter
    // and otherwise pass through to built-in formatting logic.
    println!("{}", FmtAdapter(17));
}

Ah, yeah. Sorry, had the wrong mental model for the blanket impl.

I think we're in agreement about the orphan rule; I was saying it's the classic solution to the orphan problem (which we have with just using Display).

So +1 on this. I'd hold off on KernelFormatter, though, as it needs its own justification.

Alice Ryhl said:

/// Does not assume that `write_str` is passed values containing valid utf-8.
unsafe trait KernelWrite: core::fmt::Write {
    fn write_bytes(&mut self, byt: &[u8]) -> fmt::Result {
        self.write_str(unsafe { core::str::from_utf8_unchecked(byt) })
    }
}

I don't understand how this is sound
and I don't understand why KernelFormatter::new is unsafe
&str only has utf-8 validity as a library invariant, so if we don't call methods that assume it, it's okay
does fmt::Formatter::write_str guarantee to not use methods depending on utf8?
The unsafe impl KernelWrite for X is where that guarantee is made.
it's an unsafe trait
right

but what about

Alice Ryhl said:

    fn write_bytes(&mut self, byt: &[u8]) -> fmt::Result {
        self.inner.write_str(unsafe { core::str::from_utf8_unchecked(byt) })
    }
in the KernelFormatter impl block
Formatter is a wrapper around &dyn mut Write. The KernelFormatter type comes with the invariant that the &dyn mut Write implements KernelWrite.
@Alice Ryhl would you like me to incorporate FmtAdapter + KernelDisplay into the CStr series? Would that unblock it, from your perspective?
It would unblock it, yes
@Alice Ryhl I started looking at this - I think we would need KernelDebug, KernelHex, and every other formatting trait. Does that still sound appealing?
That's fine
I think we can probably still use normal Hex? I don't think there'll be a type that needs to output non-UTF8 for hex.
I guess we could say that FmtAdapter only implements Hex if the inner type implements KernelDebug and Hex.

I think I am missing something. 

pub struct FmtAdapter<T>(pub T);

macro_rules! impl_fmt_adapter {
    ($($trait:ident),* $(,)?) => {
        $(
            impl<T: core::fmt::$trait> core::fmt::$trait for $crate::str::FmtAdapter<T> {
                fn fmt(&self, f: &mut core::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> core::fmt::Result {
                    let Self(t) = self;
                    core::fmt::$trait::fmt(t, f)
                }
            }
        )*
    };
}

impl_fmt_adapter!(Display, Debug, LowerHex, UpperHex, Octal, Binary, Pointer, LowerExp, UpperExp);

trait KernelDisplay {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result;
}

impl<T: KernelDisplay> core::fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<T> {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        let Self(t) = self;
        t.fmt(f)
    }
}

error[E0119]: conflicting implementations of trait `core::fmt::Display` for type `str::FmtAdapter<_>`
   --> rust/kernel/str.rs:949:13
    |
946 | / macro_rules! impl_fmt_adapter {
947 | |     ($($trait:ident,)*) => {
948 | |         $(
949 | |             impl<T: core::fmt::$trait> core::fmt::$trait for $crate::str::FmtAdapter<T> {
    | |             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ conflicting implementation for `str::FmtAdapter<_>`
...   |
956 | |     };
957 | | }
    | |_- in this expansion of `impl_fmt_adapter!`
958 |
959 | / impl_fmt_adapter!(
960 | |     Display,
961 | |     Debug,
962 | |     LowerHex,
...   |
968 | |     UpperExp,
969 | | );
    | |_- in this macro invocation
...
975 |   impl<T: KernelDisplay> core::fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<T> {
    |   ----------------------------------------------------------- first implementation here

error: aborting due to 1 previous error
You're implementing Display twice, both with the macro and manually.
shouldn't the impl_fmt_adapter macro use T: ::kernel::fmt::$trait instead?
If we make duals for all the traits, then yes. Is that what we want?
Duals is fine with me.
If what Gary said above is true, then we'd need some custom and others not, but I think having duals is fine too
I don't understand how we would avoid having duals for every single one. This isn't about non-UTF8 data.
impl<T: Hex> Hex for FmtAdapter<T> {}?

Ah right, of course. OK, I think this works, though it is slightly convoluted.

pub struct FmtAdapter<T>(pub T);

macro_rules! impl_fmt_adapter {
    ($($trait:ident),* $(,)?) => {
        $(
            impl<T: fmt::$trait> fmt::$trait for $crate::str::FmtAdapter<T> {
                fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
                    let Self(t) = self;
                    fmt::$trait::fmt(t, f)
                }
            }
        )*
    };
}

impl_fmt_adapter!(Debug, LowerHex, UpperHex, Octal, Binary, Pointer, LowerExp, UpperExp);

trait KernelDisplay {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result;
}

impl<T: fmt::Display> KernelDisplay for T {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        fmt::Display::fmt(self, f)
    }
}

impl<T: KernelDisplay> fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<T> {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        let Self(t) = self;
        KernelDisplay::fmt(t, f)
    }
}

/// A convenience alias for [`core::format_args`].
#[macro_export]
macro_rules! fmt {
    ($fmt:expr) => { format_args!($fmt) };
    ($fmt:expr, $($arg:expr),*) => { format_args!($fmt, $( $crate::str::FmtAdapter(&$arg) ),*) };
}
wait won't this completely break format strings?
so if I write pr_info!("{my_var}"), then the my_var is never seen by our custom fmt macro?
Duh. Yep, great point.
maybe there is a way to hook into that, but otherwise we'd either have to give up on them, or ask for a workaround that might take a while...

You can have a proc macro detect that and add

let my_var = FmtAdapter(&my_var);
It could even be format_args!("{my_var}", my_var=FmtAdapter(&my_var)), but yeah, needs to be a proc macro. :(

Well, I spent a bunch of time trying this, and I failed. The two approaches are:

  • generate a scope with a preamble, like @Benno Lossin suggested, e.g.:
{
  let my_var = FmtAdapter(&my_var);
  fmt!("{my_var}");
}

this doesn't work because core::format_args returns a value borrowed from its arguments, and the result tries to outlive the scope.

  • directly declare the named placeholders as I suggested above, e.g.:
format_args!("{my_var}", my_var=FmtAdapter(&my_var))

This also doesn't work; the compiler error is something like cannot find value my_var in this scope. This seems to have something to do with macro expansion rules that I don't quite understand.

Code: https://github.com/tamird/linux/tree/fmtadapter

If you have the macro generate a let statement rather than evaluating to an expression, then I think it can work. You expand to

let my_var = FmtAdapter(&my_var);
let args = fmt!("{my_var}");

This prevents use in expression location, but maybe that's fine.

And then pr_warn! can still work like usual.

{
    kernel_format_args!(args = ("{my_var}"));
    pr_warn_c_code(args);
}
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/f7819f7988f9c7292315c522d65301ced5cfa7db/rust/kernel/print.rs#L152
Lol that's terrible. I guess you have to generate the match in the macro and include the body as an argument to the macro.
what about making FmtAdapter repr(transparent) and doing a ref-to-ref transmute?
If that works, then go for it.
No, doesn't work, because the macro still ends up taking a ref to the ref
@Alice Ryhl do you understand why format_args!("{my_var}", my_var=FmtAdapter(&my_var)) doesn't work in a proc macro?
My guess is that it does work but only when used with that match trick.
How does the match trick help, given the error is cannot find value my_var in this scope?
is macro_rules involved here?
could be macro hygiene

initially yes, but even removing macro_rules e.g.:

diff --git a/rust/kernel/str.rs b/rust/kernel/str.rs
index 37e5756e72ad..a02eb3db227c 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/str.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/str.rs
@@ -576,6 +576,7 @@ macro_rules! c_str {
 #[expect(clippy::items_after_test_module)]
 mod tests {
     use super::*;
+    use crate::prelude::fmt;

     struct String(CString);

@@ -593,12 +594,6 @@ fn deref(&self) -> &str {
         }
     }

-    macro_rules! format {
-        ($($f:tt)*) => ({
-            &*String::from_fmt(kernel::prelude::fmt!($($f)*))
-        })
-    }
-
     const ALL_ASCII_CHARS: &str =
         "\\x01\\x02\\x03\\x04\\x05\\x06\\x07\\x08\\x09\\x0a\\x0b\\x0c\\x0d\\x0e\\x0f\
         \\x10\\x11\\x12\\x13\\x14\\x15\\x16\\x17\\x18\\x19\\x1a\\x1b\\x1c\\x1d\\x1e\\x1f \
@@ -641,13 +636,19 @@ fn test_cstr_as_str_unchecked() {
     #[test]
     fn test_cstr_display() {
         let hello_world = CStr::from_bytes_with_nul(b"hello, world!\0").unwrap();
-        assert_eq!(format!("{hello_world}"), "hello, world!");
+        assert_eq!(&*String::from_fmt(fmt!("{hello_world}")), "hello, world!");
         let non_printables = CStr::from_bytes_with_nul(b"\x01\x09\x0a\0").unwrap();
-        assert_eq!(format!("{non_printables}"), "\\x01\\x09\\x0a");
+        assert_eq!(
+            &*String::from_fmt(fmt!("{non_printables}")),
+            "\\x01\\x09\\x0a"
+        );

I still get the same error

error[E0425]: cannot find value `hello_world` in this scope
   --> ../rust/kernel/str.rs:639:39
    |
639 |         assert_eq!(&*String::from_fmt(fmt!("{hello_world}")), "hello, world!");
    |                                       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ not found in this scope
    |
    = note: this error originates in the macro `fmt` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)
No clue. I'd have to see the impl or output to say more.

impl linked above - output:

core :: format_args!
("{hello_world}", hello_world = :: kernel :: str :: FmtAdapter(hello_world))
No clue. Could not reproduce trying something simple in the playground.
could you link it?
https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=0a23c8ac990525e179e232b687fc26c0
oh. yeah, i tried this much too.

i fear this is a dead end - format_args is always used as a value, so having a block in there is a non-starter. you could do something like you suggested, passing in the match but more general, but that is also awful
this does seem to be a hygiene issue fwiw
I don't think having a block is a non-starter. Nobody uses format_args! directly, and we don't have to do anything weird to the actual printing macros.
could this be due to the mixed_site span? https://github.com/tamird/linux/commit/9b6f6d952f5257bd03720e649203c88285bf6be6#diff-6490e66e920d4cd77b5cacdf2ed30978afaabca3303d8f1f00f7f1f709e17e07R74
It could - but what should it be instead? mixed_site is the most lax, as far as I can tell.
can you try call_site
same behavior

Tamir Duberstein said:

It could - but what should it be instead? mixed_site is the most lax, as far as I can tell.

the docs say:

A span that represents macro_rules hygiene, and sometimes resolves at the macro definition site (local variables...

Tamir Duberstein said:

same behavior

hmm I'm curious which one it's referring to, so the one on the lhs or the rhs or in the string literal
we can give them different spans, if you think it matters
no that's not what I mean
can you try instead of creating a new span, using the span of eg the string literal itself?
sure
same result.

diff --git a/rust/macros/fmt.rs b/rust/macros/fmt.rs
index 7814d4f6ed6d..ef5f6632ba9e 100644
--- a/rust/macros/fmt.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/fmt.rs
@@ -46,6 +46,7 @@ pub(crate) fn fmt(input: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
         }
     };

+    let span = first_opt.as_ref().map(|tt| tt.span()).unwrap();
     let mut args = TokenStream::from_iter(first_opt);
     {
         let mut current = TokenStream::new();
@@ -71,7 +72,7 @@ pub(crate) fn fmt(input: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
     }

     for name in names {
-        let name = Ident::new(name, Span::mixed_site());
+        let name = Ident::new(name, Span::call_site());
         args.extend(quote! {
             , #name = ::kernel::str::FmtAdapter(#name)
         });
eh, whoops. sec
hey it worked!
nice
seems like it was hygiene after all :)
yep
you probably are aware of this, but you'll need to implement { escaping, so pr_info!("{{abc}}"); should print {abc}
and what happens when a user does pr_info!("{a}", a = b);?

I wasn't aware, but thanks - I will do that. While I have you: any ideas on how to test this?

pr_info!("{a}", a = b);

this is already handled, it will become

pr_info!("{a}", a = FmtAdapter(&b));

Tamir Duberstein said:

I wasn't aware, but thanks - I will do that. While I have you: any ideas on how to test this?

hmm what exactly do you mean by test? we could try to write a format string to a string and then check the result?

pr_info!("{a}", a = b);

this is already handled, it will become

pr_info!("{a}", a = FmtAdapter(&b));

ah that's good, but wouldnt this be better?:

pr_info!("{a}", a = FmtAdapter(b));

you mean without the pretzel? that is actually what comes out now.

what I mean by test: ideally assert on token stream in vs token stream out
ah you mean testing the expanded code. I use the macrotest crate for pin-init, but that of course needs cargo...
we do have the rsi target for expanding code, so there are ways to make rustcoutput the expanded version
but we pretty sure don't have support for that in the build system
what is the rsi target?

from make help:

  dir/file.rsi    - Build macro expanded source, similar to C preprocessing.
                    Run with RUSTFMT=n to skip reformatting if needed.
                    The output is not intended to be compilable.
essentially cargo expand
but I don't know the internals, so it might still be a bit different
oh amazing, did not know
but yeah, doesn't help with automated testing
yeah but this means that the expansion logic is exposed from rustc
so in theory, we could have these kinds of tests
building e.g. rust/kernel/str.rsi produces a bunch of compiler diagnostics from the attempt to compile it, but i don't find the source anywhere =/
ah yeah, so there are 2 problems that I can see, the first one I knew about: .rsi targets only work when there are no compile errors. the second problem is that it doesn't add the kernel prelude

ah it's just

quiet_cmd_rustc_rsi_rs = $(RUSTC_OR_CLIPPY_QUIET) $(quiet_modtag) $@
cmd_rustc_rsi_rs = \
$(rust_common_cmd) -Zunpretty=expanded $< >$@; \
command -v $(RUSTFMT) >/dev/null && $(RUSTFMT) $@

and it feeds it right to the compiler -- but anyway, this is pretty unergonomic, writing this test would be nontrivial
yeah

There's a new hitch. This all worked fine when all the types involved did actually impl Display, even without the adapter. Once I bring in core::ffi::CStr I'm back to weird errors:

OUT:
core :: format_args!
("{hello_world}", hello_world = :: kernel :: str :: FmtAdapter(& hello_world))

iii.error[E0277]: `core::ffi::CStr` doesn't implement `core::fmt::Display`
   --> ../rust/kernel/str.rs:413:32
    |
413 |             &*String::from_fmt(crate::prelude::fmt!($($f)*))
    |                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ `core::ffi::CStr` cannot be formatted with the default formatter
...
450 |         assert_eq!(format!("{hello_world}"), "hello, world!");
    |                    ------------------------ in this macro invocation
    |
    = help: the trait `core::fmt::Display` is not implemented for `core::ffi::CStr`
    = note: in format strings you may be able to use `{:?}` (or {:#?} for pretty-print) instead
    = help: the trait `core::fmt::Display` is implemented for `str::FmtAdapter<T>`
    = note: this error originates in the macro `core::format_args` which comes from the expansion of the macro `format` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

so it now seems the span of the string inside the macro is wrong??
what are the impls on FmtAdapter? did you add the correct forwarding for KernelDisplay?
the error doesn't seem like it has to do anything with hygiene

Read the error carefully, it's saying CStr isn't Display, which is what it's trying to render.
It's also saying " `core::fmt::Display` is implemented for `str::FmtAdapter<T>` "

so this error looks to me like something is resolving hello_world to the outer scope instead of to this local parameter
ah I'm too tired... I'll take another look tomorrow.

FWIW I tried removing all uses of quote! to have as precise control over spans as possible -- still no love.

Updated https://github.com/tamird/linux/tree/fmtadapter with a test that reproduces the issue.
Any appetite for this, anyone? :)
For what?

If you pull down my code linked above, you'll see quite perplexing failures:
the macro emits to stderr

:: core :: format_args!
("{}\n", :: kernel :: str :: FmtAdapter(& not_display))

but the compiler complains:

error[E0277]: `NotDisplay` doesn't implement `Display`
    --> rust/doctests_kernel_generated.rs:4046:1

4046 | print_macro!(format_strings::NOTICE, false, "{}\n", not_display);

so somehow the macro's rewrite isn't working, or something?

        fn main() {

                match format_args!("{0}\n",

                            crate::__LOG_PREFIX, args);

            }
            _doctest_main____rust_kernel_print_rs_143_0()
        }

Yeah, I think it is. I think I've fixed it now!

diff --git a/rust/kernel/str.rs b/rust/kernel/str.rs
index 19b8aac7fe4c..1fd89f223070 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/str.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/str.rs
@@ -993,9 +993,9 @@ fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {

 impl_fmt_adapter!(Debug, LowerHex, UpperHex, Octal, Binary, Pointer, LowerExp, UpperExp);

-impl<T: KernelDisplay> fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<T> {
+impl<T: KernelDisplay> fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<&T> {
     fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
         let Self(t) = self;
-        KernelDisplay::fmt(t, f)
+        KernelDisplay::fmt(*t, f)
     }

the relevant impls

pub trait KernelDisplay {
    /// Formats the value using the given formatter, like [`fmt::Display::fmt`].
    ///
    /// This method is invoked by the [`fmt!`] macro through the [`FmtAdapter`] wrapper.
    ///
    /// [`fmt!`]: crate::prelude::fmt!
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result;
}

impl<T: fmt::Display> KernelDisplay for T {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        fmt::Display::fmt(self, f)
    }
}

/// Internal adapter used to route formatting through [`KernelDisplay`] inside the [`fmt!`] macro.
///
/// It is inserted automatically by the [`fmt!`] macro and is not meant to be used directly.
///
/// [`fmt!`]: crate::prelude::fmt!
#[doc(hidden)]
pub struct FmtAdapter<T>(pub T);

macro_rules! impl_fmt_adapter {
    ($($trait:ident),* $(,)?) => {
        $(
            impl<T: fmt::$trait> fmt::$trait for $crate::str::FmtAdapter<T> {
                fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
                    let Self(t) = self;
                    fmt::$trait::fmt(t, f)
                }
            }
        )*
    };
}

impl_fmt_adapter!(Debug, LowerHex, UpperHex, Octal, Binary, Pointer, LowerExp, UpperExp);

impl<T: KernelDisplay> fmt::Display for FmtAdapter<&T> {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        let Self(t) = self;
        KernelDisplay::fmt(*t, f)
    }
}

Does this fix it?

diff --git a/rust/macros/fmt.rs b/rust/macros/fmt.rs
index 2f4b9f6e2211..8354abd54502 100644
--- a/rust/macros/fmt.rs
+++ b/rust/macros/fmt.rs
@@ -67,7 +67,7 @@ pub(crate) fn fmt(input: TokenStream) -> TokenStream {
                     }
                     (None, acc)
                 })();
-                args.extend(quote_spanned!(first_span => #lhs #adapter(&#rhs)));
+                args.extend(quote_spanned!(first_span => #lhs #adapter(&(#rhs))));
             }
         };
